coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal pollution is life-threatening, the detection of heavy metals is crucial to human health and environment. In this work, AIE featured LA-stabilized gold nanoclusters (NL-AuNCs) were designed and fabricated by modulating precursors in various reaction conditions, and utilized as an efficient fluorescent sensor for portable detecting Ag+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. The NL-AuNCs exhibited intense red fluorescence at 625 nm via the secondary reducing agent N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC), achieving a quantum yield (QY) of 15.7 %. Notably, the introduction of Ag+ enhanced the red fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs on account of the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) process, rendering NL-AuNCs uniquely capable of detecting Ag+ with the ultralow detection limit of 1.3 nM within a wide concentration range of 0.002–200 μM. Furthermore, the handheld intelligent sensing strategy was constructed by integrating the smartphone App, which enabled swift portable monitoring and convenient readout by visual assessment of color evolution. The acceptable recoveries of 94.6 % to 114.9 % were attained from testing real water, indicating the excellent environmental tolerance of NL-AuNCs, which allowed the possibility for practical in-situ detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of NL-AuNCs synthesized under different reaction conditions: various concentrations of (a) NAC (fixed the concentration of Au3+ and LA) and (b) LA (fixed the concentration of Au3+ and NAC), and different (c) temperature, and (d) reacting time (All the insets were fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0, where I referred to the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs synthesized under different conditions, and I0 corresponding to the fluorescence intensity of blank).

TEM images of NL-AuNCs synthesized with different solvents, (a) ultra-pure water, (b) ethanol, (insets: particle size distribution, HRTEM, and FFT patterns of single particle), (c) FT-IR spectra of NAC (red line) and AuNCs (black line), (d) full XPS spectrum of AuNCs, and high-resolution XPS of (e) Au 4f and (f) S 2P. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission of NL-AuNCs, (corresponding images under day-light (left) and UV-lamp (right)), fluorescence spectra of NL-AuNCs (b) under different excitation wavelength, and (c) synthesized in ultra-pure water and ethanol, respectively, (d) 1931 CIE chromaticity coordinates.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NL-AuNCs mixed with different kinds of common metal cations, (b) corresponding selectivity histograms (insert: images of AuNCs mixed with different metal cations under UV-light), (c) fluorescence spectra of NL-AuNCs mixed with Ag+ of different concentrations (images of NL-AuNCs mixed with increased concentration of Ag+ under UV-lamp), (d) linear relationship between I0/I and Ag+ concentration, (e) color recognition of selectivity by smartphone, (f) linear relationship between the RGB values (R/B) and the Ag+ concentration (inset: the imaging of the corresponding color evolution under UV lamp captured by iPhone 14 Pro Max).

(a) UV–vis absorption of NL-AuNCs and NL-AuNCs + Ag+, (b) Fluorescence lifetime of NL-AuNCs and NL-AuNCs + Ag+, TEM images of (c) NL-AuNCs, and (d) NL-AuNCs with Ag+ (insert: particle size distribution, HRTEM, and FFT patterns of single particle).
The strategy of the synthesis of fluorescent NL-AuNCs, smartphone-assisted color analysis and detection of Ag+ in real sample water.
Test results of Ag+ in the real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: The controllable fabrication of AIE gold nanoclusters and utilizing as portable ultrasensitive detection sensor for silver ions via smartphone

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal pollution is life-threatening, the detection of heavy metals is crucial to human health and environment. In this work, AIE featured LA-stabilized gold nanoclusters (NL-AuNCs) were designed and fabricated by modulating precursors in various reaction conditions, and utilized as an efficient fluorescent sensor for portable detecting Ag+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. The NL-AuNCs exhibited intense red fluorescence at 625 nm via the secondary reducing agent N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC), achieving a quantum yield (QY) of 15.7 %. Notably, the introduction of Ag+ enhanced the red fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs on account of the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) process, rendering NL-AuNCs uniquely capable of detecting Ag+ with the ultralow detection limit of 1.3 nM within a wide concentration range of 0.002–200 μM. Furthermore, the handheld intelligent sensing strategy was constructed by integrating the smartphone App, which enabled swift portable monitoring and convenient readout by visual assessment of color evolution. The acceptable recoveries of 94.6 % to 114.9 % were attained from testing real water, indicating the excellent environmental tolerance of NL-AuNCs, which allowed the possibility for practical in-situ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a high-profile functional material, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) possess a pronounced quantum size effect due to their ultra-small size (1–3 nm), causing unique fluorescence characteristics [1,2], which can be utilized in the research of optical devices, fluorescence probes, bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, photocatalysis, etc. The advancement of AuNCs has created a tunable strategy for fabricating functional materials for their controllable composition, unique optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility [3]. However, the controllable synthesis of AuNCs through design is one of the major scientific and challenging goals, due to the quantum yield (QY) of AuNCs still falling behind that of traditional organic fluorescent molecules. Many reported AuNCs exhibited relatively low QYs and low molar extinction coefficients [4], leading to the weak signal ratios of AuNCs in sensing platforms [5], which severely inhibited their development and application in various realms. Fortunately, the discovery of a novel luminescent phenomenon known as Aggregation-Induced Emission (AIE) offers enhanced emission of AuNCs by the efficient restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM), reducing the non-radiative transitions, has pioneered a new avenue for the research of fluorescent materials [6]. Contrary to the conventional Aggregation-Caused Quenching (ACQ) effect, AIE refers to the remarkable enhancement of light emission intensity observed in certain molecules or materials when they are in an aggregated state [7,8]. The uniqueness of AIE AuNCs stems from their RIM mechanism, wherein under aggregated conditions, the intramolecular motions are constrained, leading to a reduction in non-radiative transitions, and an enhancement in the efficiency of radiative transitions consequently [9]. With the intriguing AIE traits and rich surface chemistry, the AIE featured AuNCs unprecedentedly exhibit promising in sensing research field. Notably, the incorporation of the AIE mechanism into the design, fabrication and utilization of AuNCs not only effectively boosts fluorescence QY to conquer the effect of background, but also fortifies their stability and functionality, paving the way to explore efficient practical application. Thus, the AIE featured AuNCs with high QY was expected to be attained to develop their sensing performance.

On the other hand, heavy metals cause serious threat to human health and the environment [10]. Silver (Ag) boasts remarkable optical, electrical conductivity, and potent antibacterial properties [11], however, the widespread utilization of silver has also occasioned the emergence of environmental challenges, the discharge of industrial wastewater containing excessive concentrations of Ag+, poses a significant threat to the ecological balance and potential health risks to humans [12]. Ag+ can disrupt homeostasis and metabolism, leading to various ailments, and their interaction with thiol groups in proteins and nucleic acids can result in the rapid deactivation of enzymes, ultimately causing lung damage, renal failure, nerve damage, and even fatal consequences [13,14]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) stipulated the maximum allowable value of Ag+ in drinking water were 0.36 μM and 0.93 μM, respectively [15]. In view of this, implementing an effective sensing method is crucial for minimizing the risks associated with Ag+, and ensuring the safety of food and environment. Though, numerous conventional analysis techniques including ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry [16], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [17], and inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy [18], have been adopted for heavy metals, unfortunately, the majority of these techniques suffer from several limitations of complicated procedure, costly equipment, time-consuming processes, professional operation, and complex result readout, which restricted the swift in-situ detection. Consequently, there is a pressing need to investigate and develop convenient portable sensors posing portability to meet the requirements of rapid response, ease of operation, and high selectivity and sensitivity [19,20]. And, designing long-lasting yet high-efficiency effective and accurate methods is an everlasting goal for environmental and human health.

To date, the main analytical materials that can be devoted to fluorescent detection are noble metal nanoparticles [21], metal organic frameworks [22], carbon quantum dots [23], up-conversion nanomaterials [24] and metal nanoclusters [25]. Among them, as robust fluorescent sensors, AuNCs become a promising candidate with easily recognizable signals, steady sensing performance, swift response, and portable manipulation [26]. For example, Liang et al. have reported the construction of AIE featured thiolactic acid-capped AuNCs with near‑infrared emission and detected Ag+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM [27]. Meng et al. reported a high-throughput fluorescence sensor array based on three kinds of AuNCs to identify and quantify the seven heavy metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Co2+, As3+ and Hg2+), the results achieved detecting Ag+ with satisfactory results with LOD of 8.46 nM [28]. In summary, the construction of a simple, rapid, highly sensitive, and selective fluorescence sensor based on AIE AuNCs holds immense promise for the in-situ detection of Ag+.

Inspired by these, this study successfully synthesized red gold nanoclusters (NL-AuNCs) employing the one-pot reduction method, utilizing the secondary reducing agent N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) and the stabilizing and reducing agent DL-lipoic acid (LA), as schematically outlined in Scheme 1 . The introduction of Ag+ fostered the aggregation of dispersed NL-AuNCs, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs through the AIE phenomenon. Furthermore, the inherent capability of NL-AuNCs to reduce Ag+, effectively eliminating surface defects, results in an additional boost to the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs. The detection of Ag+ can be rendered highly sensitive through utilization of the fluorescence enhancement principle. Furthermore, by utilizing a smartphone-assisted color recognition App, this sensor demonstrated its promising potential in the visual and portable detection for Ag+, as evident from the distinct color changes observed under UV light. A more promising platform for precisely detecting Ag+ in ambient water was made available by this strategy, serving as a benchmark for subsequent monitoring of Ag+. The NL-AuNCs demonstrated superior sensitivity, rapidity, convenience, and ultralow detection limit, expected to possess considerable potential for Ag+ detection applications. This work provided a strategy to fabricate high QY AuNCs and further served as a convenient accurate and flexible sensor for application in environment protection.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O) was brought from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (USA). N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) and DL-lipoic acid (LA) were purchased from Aladdin Inc. (Shanghai China). Various metal salts (MgNO3·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, Mn(Ac)2·4H2O, Hg(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, PbNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, K2Cr2O7, CuSO4·5H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Co(Ac)2·6H2O, AgNO3, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O and CaCl2·2H2O) were obtained by Beijing Chemical Reagents. All solutions were prepared to use distilled water (18.2 MΩ·cm) and all chemicals were analytically pure.

LENGGUANG F98 (China) spectrophotometers were applied for the fluorescence spectral characteristics measurements. Characterization of the morphologies of NL-AuNCs were tested on an HT-7800 (Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using a JASCOFT/IR-420 (Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained using a UV-2500 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZUM, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (England). The fluorescence lifetime of samples was measured on an FLS980 (USA) spectrofluorometer time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC, UK).

Prior to utilization, all glassware was immersed in aqua regia and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water to ensure a contaminant-free environment. Firstly, 2.96 mL of freshly prepared HAuCl4·3H2O solution (24.55 mM, 11.49 mL C2H5OH), 0.026 g of LA solution (0.25 M, 0.45 mL C2H5OH) and 0.02 g of NAC solution (0.25 M, 0.45 mL C2H5OH) were mixed together under stirring at room temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, the mixture was assembled in a reflux reaction and heated at 70 °C in an oil bath for 24 h, resulting in a milky white solution. Following, the NL-AuNCs were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate the precipitates, and then appropriately redispersed in ethanol. Eventually, the solution was left for a week followed by centrifuging again, and the supernatant was collected. For subsequent characterization and utilization, the NL-AuNCs solution can be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Moreover, the reaction temperatures, reaction durations, and ratios of reactant concentrations were systematically modulated to obtain optimal synthetic conditions.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference towards Ag+, NL-Au NCs were mixed with various metal cation solutions (including Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ag+, Cd2+, Ba2+ and Hg2+) and then the fluorescence spectra of samples were tested and compared. Subsequently, the standard solution of Ag+ with gradient concentrations 0.002–200 μM were prepared seriously. Then the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs with various concentrations of Ag+ samples were collected and analyzed to estimate the sensitivity.

To implement the readable visual detection, the images reflecting their evaluation by different metal ions and various concentrations of Ag+ were collected and treated. Utilizing the smartphone App (Color Grab, Loomatix) for color extraction and recognition, the fluorescent images were converted into the corresponding digital Red (R), Green (G), and Blue (B) information. After optimizing and evaluating the relationship between the R, G, B values and Ag+ concentration, the red-to-blue channel ratio (R/B) was set as the relevant parameter for quantitative detection of Ag+ and each value was measured three times to eliminate errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of Ag+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility and accuracy of the NL-AuNCs towards Ag+ in various real water samples, we selected tap water from our laboratory, as well as river water from the Songhua River and the Aershan River. Prior to analysis, these samples were pretreated through a 0.45 µm microfilter to eliminate large impurities, ensuring the purity of the water samples. Subsequently, NL-AuNCs solution was mixed with two different concentrations (0.35 μM and 1.0 μM) of Ag+ solution and incubated at room temperature for 5 min, further the fluorescence emission spectra of all samples were recorded. Finally, the recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to assess the accuracy and precision of the measurements. All the experiments were tested three times to reduce possible errors.

In this study, as a fluorescent sensor, NL-AuNCs were synthesized by using a one-pot reaction with NAC and LA as precursors (Scheme 1). Serving as a promising template for synthesizing AuNCs, the LA with rich functional carboxyl groups in its molecule structure exhibited extremely strong coordination abilities. The disulfide bond in LA endows it reducing capabilities, and the presence of the S-H bond in NAC is forcefully reductive [29], which achieved the precise formation of tiny AuNCs, and with highly stable photostability and QY. Consequently, series of experiments were manipulated and demonstrated that the utilization of LA as a stabilizing and reducing agent, integrating with the involvement of NAC for secondary reduction, resulted in the production of red-emitting NL-AuNCs with prominent fluorescence traits. In this work, NAC was employed as the secondary reducing agent, integrating with LA, and the achievement of fabrication of NL-AuNCs with high QY and AIE features can be accomplished via the synergistic effect of NAC and LA. The color of NL-AuNCs solution was milk white under the sunlight and emitted red emission under 365 nm UV light with the emission at 625 nm.

Firstly, the roles of LA and NAC in the synthesis of NL-AuNCs were investigated by modulating as the precursors in reactions. The AuNCs synthesized by LA or NAC separately exhibited weak intensity, especially the fluorescence of NAC-AuNCs (Fig. S1). When NAC was utilized as a secondary reducing agent, a substantial increase in the fluorescence intensity of LN-AuNCs was observed, which enhanced more than 4 folds than LA-AuNCs, indicating the initiation of formation of cluster nucleation was accomplished by coexistence of two precursors (Fig. S1). Evidently, NL-AuNCs exhibited a dominant advantage in fluorescence intensity than LA-AuNCs and NAC-AuNCs, manifesting both LA and NAC played indispensable roles in the synthesis process, the synergies of NAC and LA suppress the assembly of AuNCs through aurophilic attraction, promoted the formation of AuNCs, and further facilitating the fluorescent properties and stability of AuNCs.

To optimize the synthesis of NL-AuNCs, the ratio of precursors, reaction time, and reaction temperature were investigated systematically (Fig. 1 ). As observed in Fig. 1a–b, the fluorescence intensity progressively rises and culminates at an Au/NAC/LA concentration ratio of 1:50:50, subsequently exhibiting a decline with further additions of NAC and LA. The fluorescence intensity, attained at the aforementioned conditions was assessed to confirm the optimality. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity was observed to augment with increasing reaction temperature, peaking at 70 °C (Fig. 1c–d), these results further illustrated that the fluorescence intensity intensifies with the escalation of both reaction temperature and time, ultimately attaining optimal conditions at 70 °C and 24 h. Hence, the concentration ratio of Au/NAC/LA at 1:50:50, temperature of 70 °C and reaction time of 24 h were determined as the optimal condition for the synthesis of NL-AuNCs, yielding a fluorescence QY of 15.7 %, which was a higher result in red emissive AuNCs. Ultimately, it was discovered that the incorporation of Ag+ significantly amplifies the fluorescence of NL-AuNCs, enabling the feasibility of sensing Ag+ quantificationally by utilizing NL-AuNCs as a fluorescent sensor.

The morphology, particle size, surface functional groups, and composition of the proposed red emissive NL-AuNCs were comprehensively characterized and analyzed by using TEM, FT-IR and XPS. The TEM images of the NL-AuNCs exhibited a spherical monodisperse morphology and maintained a good degree of dispersion in different solvents (Fig. 2 a–b). By measuring randomly 100 selected particles, the average particle sizes were determined at 2.4 ± 0.5 nm (solvent: C2H5OH) and 1.84 ± 0.07 nm (solvent: H2O). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging revealed that the lattice plane of 0.235 nm corresponds to the Au (111) plane, aligning with previously reported literature, and further validating the precise structural characteristics of the proposed NL-AuNCs [30].

The ability to bond between NAC and NL-AuNCs was investigated using FT-IR spectra. As delineated in Fig. 2c (red curve), the observed absorption peaks provided insights into the vibrational modes of various functional groups present within the structure. The peak at 3369 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of NL-AuNCs [31]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks observed at 2890 cm−1 and 2809 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–H stretching vibration [32,33]. Additionally, the absorption peaks located at 1717 cm−1 and 1534 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the CO bond in the amide I band and the N–H bending vibration in the amide II band, respectively [34,35]. These findings highlighted the amide linkage within the structure, which played a crucial role in determining the functional properties of NL-AuNCs. Importantly, the distinct absorption peak (red curve) observed at 2543 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic of the S-H stretching vibration which also played a crucial role in the synthesis of AuNCs [36], indicating the disruption of the S-H bond during the formation of the Au-S bond (black curve). This transformation suggested that the rich reducing functional group S-H in the NAC structure facilitated the binding with the Au core to form stable NL-AuNCs with the Au-S bond wrapped on their surface. Overall, the FT-IR spectra provided valuable insights into the chemical bonding and functional group composition of ligands and their interaction with AuNCs, contributing to a deep understanding of the structure and properties of NL-AuNCs.

To survey the chemical composition, the XPS spectra of NL-AuNCs were meticulously analyzed in Fig. 2d–f. The surface composition of NL-AuNCs revealed the presence of Au, C, N, O, and S, indicating the enrichment of LA and NAC functional groups on their surfaces (Fig. 2d). The coexistence of Au (0) and Au (+) within the Au 4f track was verified through the binding energies (BE) at 87.9 eV and 84.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 2e). Notably, the BE at 87.9 eV confirmed the presence of Au (0) [37]. The BE at 84.4 eV, situating between 84.0 eV for Au (0) and 85.2 eV for Au (I), underscored the coexistence of both Au (0) and Au (I) on NL-AuNCs surface [38]. Furthermore, the spectrum displayed peaks at 162.8 eV and 163.8 eV, denoting the formation of the Au-S bond (Fig. 2f) [39]. Since the disulfide bond of lipoic acid was easy to realize ring-opening polymerization reaction, it can provide active sites for Au and easier to form stable Au-S bonds [40]. According to pertinent literature, the enhancement of fluorescence stability and fluorescence intensity for AuNCs was attributed to the chemical bond formed between thiol groups in NAC and Au (+) on the surface of AuNCs [41]. The presence of these bonds, anchored to the surface of AuNCs, formed a stable protective layer, facilitating their sensing performance. According to XPS and FT-IR measurements, it was observed that under the protective influence of LA, the NL-AuNCs, prepared using NAC as a secondary reducing agent, exhibited a rich presence of LA and NAC functional groups on their surfaces, which contributed significantly to their high water dispersibility and stability. Thus, it can be inferred that the formation of Au-S bonds in the NL-AuNCs may stem from the bonding between Au (+) present in AuNCs and thiol groups inherent in the precursor of NAC. The Au-S bond, wrapping around the surface of the Au core, further enhanced the photoluminescent intensity of AuNCs, which was also consistent with previous mention regarding the pivotal role played by NAC as a secondary reducing agent [42].

The spectral characteristics of NL-AuNCs were thoroughly investigated utilizing UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The NL-AuNCs exhibited milk white color under the sunlight and emitted a distinct red fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 3 a illustration). By employing a fluorescence spectrophotometer excited at 255 nm to capture the energy of the surface state of the AuNCs, the NL-AuNCs solution showed a pronounced emission at 625 nm, representing a large Stoke shift, which was a characteristic unique to AIE featured AuNCs [43]. There were no surface plasmon resonance absorption peaks or distinct characteristic absorption peaks observed in the UV–vis absorbance of NL-AuNCs (Fig. 3a black curve), thereby corroborating the successful synthesis of ultrasmall NL-AuNCs [44].

The fluorescence tests were conducted to investigate the impact of various excitation wavelengths ranging from 345 nm to 365 nm on the emission spectra of NL-AuNCs. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the alterations of excitation wavelength did not influence the position of the emission peak, which consistently appeared around 625 nm. This non-excitation dependence characteristic served as evidence for the synthesis of single-component and well-dispersed AuNCs [45]. Additionally, to achieve the high fluorescence intensity of AuNCs, an optimal excitation wavelength of 355 nm was identified, and the corresponding emission peak aligned well with intense red emission calculated by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE 1931) chromaticity (Fig. 3d). The fluorescence QY was calculated as 15.7 % with quinine sulphate as reference, and the lifetime of the NL-AuNCs was measured to be 4.19 μs. The above results illustrated that NL-AuNCs possessed excellent optical properties, which enabled their outstanding performance in sensing applications. Furthermore, the solvent dependence of NL-AuNCs was also investigated by testing their fluorescence intensity after being dispersed in different solvents (H2O, C2H5OH). As depicted in Fig. 3c, it was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of the NL-AuNCs remained unaffected by the choice of solvent, indicating the solvent independence of the NL-AuNCs, which characteristic reduced the reliance on specific solvents in complex detection environments.

The stability and environmental adaptability are a crucial parameter to survey the feasibility of NL-AuNCs utilized in real practical detection. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs at different pH and ionic strength was evaluated respectively. As shown in Fig. S2a, minimal changes were observed on the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs within a wide pH range of 1 to 11, indicating the excellent pH tolerance of NL-AuNCs, which allowed their application in a wide pH range. Additionally, under high salt conditions (Fig. S2b), there was negligible variation observed in the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs, confirming that high salt conditions did not significantly affect the fluorescence characteristics of AuNCs, manifesting the high salt resistance of NL-AuNCs, facilitating their utilization in a complicated environment. Consequently, these data demonstrated the excellent environment tolerance of NL-AuNCs, further supporting the XPS and FT-IR analysis. The formation of Au-S bonds at the surface of the gold nuclear effectively encapsulated the AuNCs, playing a crucial role in maintaining their stability and protective properties. This stability ensured the NL-AuNCs retained their optical properties, making them suitable for various applications where high stability and fluorescence efficiency are required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of NL-AuNCs towards Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of NL-AuNCs, protected by LA rich surficial functional groups, possessing excellent fluorescent stability, optical properties, and active sites, revealed their potential for detecting heavy metal ions. The selectivity towards Ag+ was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs with various metal cations (including Ag+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Co2+ and Ca2+) individually. The Fig. 4 a–b showed the fluorescence emission spectra of NL-AuNCs following the introduction of various metal ions into NL-AuNCs, along with corresponding histograms and UV lamp irradiation images. Compared to the blank, the red fluorescent NL-AuNCs exhibited the most significant selectivity towards Ag+. Notably, the addition of Ag+ resulted in a potent enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of NL-AuNCs. In contrast, the introduction of other ions did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the NL-AuNCs (in Fig. 4b inset), demonstrating the high selectivity of the NL-AuNCs towards Ag+. To evaluate the detection performance of Ag+ detection system based on NL-AuNCs in complex system, its anti-interference was tested. As shown in Fig. S3, in the presence of other metal ions, the fluorescence response of NL-AuNCs to Ag+ remained stable without being damaged by other metal ions. This showed that there was a unique interaction mechanism between NL-AuNCs and Ag+, which enabled the detection system to effectively eliminate the interference of other substances and accurately detect Ag+. The above analysis indicated that the NL-AuNCs can be considered as a novel “turn-on” sensor to selectively recognize the presence of Ag+.

Utilizing the NL-AuNCs for the specific detection of Ag+ via fluorescence enhancement, we delved deeper into the sensitivity of fluorescence intensity of AuNCs towards Ag+ detection. The Fig. 4c–d illustrated the correlation between fluorescence emission and Ag+ concentration across a broad range (0–200 μM). As the rising concentration of Ag+, the fluorescence spectra of NL-AuNCs exhibited a corresponding evident augmentation regularly. Simultaneously, this trend concurred with the gradual intensification of red fluorescence observed in the sample solution images under UV light irradiation (Fig. 4c insert), and it is closely related to the color trend displayed in CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram (Fig. S5). Importantly, when the Ag+ concentration falls within the range of 0.002–1 μM, a well-fitted linear relationship between I0/I ratio and Ag+ concentration can be determined. The linear equations were I0/I = −26.4x + 0.829 in the range of 0.002–0.010 μM (R2 = 0.98) and y = −0.166x + 0.585 in the range of 0.010–1.0 μM (R2 = 0.99) (Fig. 4d and S4). (Herein, I0 represents the fluorescent intensity of blank sample (NL-AuNCs), while I denotes the fluorescent intensity of NL-AuNCs mixed with Ag+). According to this linear relationship, employing the formula 3σ/k for calculation purposes (where σ is the relative standard deviation and k represents slope from the linear equation: k = 26.93), the lowest LOD was determined at 1.3 nM, which was lower than the allowance minimum value in drink water stipulated by both WHO and USEPA. Therefore, the results demonstrated that the NL-AuNCs can achieve sensitive detection at ultralow concentrations of Ag+ in strict drink water. Furthermore, comparing the performance of the proposed NL-AuNCs probe for Ag+ detection with other reported probes in Table S1 [46–48], the proposed probe exhibited superior sensitivity, highlighting its robust detection capabilities and potential as an effective sensor for Ag+ detection.

Visual detection, with its intuitive and efficient advantages, has demonstrated tremendous potential in rapid on-site evaluations across various practical applications. The utilization of robust smartphone image processing capabilities to precisely convert color information into RGB values for analysis has emerged as an efficient and indispensable innovative approach [49]. Fig. 4e illustrated the process of color recognition of a selective image through the Color Grab App on a smartphone. The color change induced by Ag+ stands out prominently with its color card undergoing a marked transition to red. This stark contrast to the consistent purple hue exhibited by other metal ions underscores the unparalleled uniqueness and heightened recognizability of Ag+ within metal ions selectivity. The corresponding color can be precisely converted into RGB values, which formed digital information of color to delineate the colorimetric evolution. Subsequently, the detection of Ag+ was evaluated through the calculation according to the reference of the R/B ratio. As shown in Fig. 4f, an excellent linear relationship between the R/B value and the concentration of Ag+ was determined, achieving an ultralow LOD of 0.5 nM, indicating an ultra-sensitive detection. Compared with the previously reported smartphone detection of Ag+ (Table S2), it was evident that the LOD achieved by our proposed method surpasses that of several existing approaches, demonstrating its superiority in sensitivity.

To assess the potential interference of smartphone camera on detection results, two other different models (Huawei P30 and Redmi K70) were selected randomly for testing. The experimental results revealed that despite slight variations in the R, G, and B values captured by different bands of smartphones, the growth trend of the ratio between R and B values remained consistent (Figs. S6 and S7). This finding suggested that the correlation between the R/B ratio and Ag+ concentration remained consistent across different smartphone platforms, indicating the variations in smartphone models can be neglected. To further verify the accuracy of this method, we conducted detection on samples with known Ag+ concentrations using images captured by different smartphones. As shown in Table S3, the analysis results demonstrated that the Ag+ concentrations calculated using different smartphones were highly consistent, confirmed the accuracy and adaptability of this method authoritatively, ensured their promising possibility of application. This innovative visual detection methodology significantly boosted convenience and practicality, providing a streamlined and efficient method for detecting Ag+.

The possible sensing mechanism underlying the detection of Ag+ by NL-AuNCs was examined to understand the sensing process deeply. Initially, the absorption spectra revealed a slight enhancement in absorbance at 330 nm upon the introduction of Ag+, devoid of the emergence of any novel peaks (Fig. 5 a). This result indicated that the observed fluorescence intensification was not attributable to the formation of a new species. The increase in absorbance may stem from the aggregation of AuNCs particles, which concurrently absorbed and dispersed the incident light, thereby changing the optical properties of the system [50]. To further validate the speculation, a comparative analysis of TEM images was conducted in both the presence and absence of Ag+. Compared to NL-AuNCs devoid of Ag+ introduction, the average particle size of NL-AuNCs with Ag+ increased from 1.8 nm to 2.8 nm, accompanied by a decrease in the degree of monodispersity (Fig. 5c-5d). This observation suggested that the diameter of the NL-AuNCs particles and their aggregation degree were enhanced accompanied with the introduction of Ag+, indicating that the fluorescence enhancement observed in the NL-AuNCs-Ag+ system was attributed to the aggregation of AuNCs particles, which precisely corroborated the mechanism of AIE [51]. To further demonstrate the AIE effect, the fluorescence lifetime measurements of NL-AuNCs in the absence and presence of Ag+ were conducted (Fig. 5b). Notably, the introduction of Ag+ into the NL-AuNCs solution elicited negligible alterations in the fluorescence decay lifetime, indicating that the interaction with Ag+ did not affect the electronic recombination process of NL-AuNCs, implying that the fluorescent emission arose from the identical fluorophores with an enhanced emission intensity. This finding underscored the AIE mechanism, that the augmentation of fluorescent emission stems from the confinement of intramolecular motions among the identical fluorophores [52]. This confinement diminished energy dissipation pathways, thereby elevating the proportion of energy released in the form of light, ultimately resulting in fluorescence enhancement. In addition, the fluorescence enhancement may also be attributed to the reducing capacity of the NL-AuNCs. Previous investigations have demonstrated the reductive capability of AuNCs towards Ag+ [53]. The HRTEM showed that the lattice plane at 0.204 nm corresponded to Ag (100) in Fig. 5d insert, demonstrating that Ag+ was reduced via Galvanic replacement reaction by Au (0) existing on the surface of AuNCs and formed deposited Ag (0), thereby eliminating the surface defects of AuNCs, leading to a substantial enhancement in their fluorescence intensity [54]. The results manifested characteristic AIE feature contributed to the sensing performance and expected promising development.

To delve deeper into the interaction mechanism involving Ag+ and NL-AuNCs, XPS testing was performed on the NL-AuNCs + Ag+ system, with the objective of determining the chemical state of Au and Ag. In the NL-AuNCs + Ag+ system, the binding energy peaks of Au were observed at 88.3 eV and 84.8 eV, respectively, the energy of Au increasing 0.4 eV with the addition of Ag+ (Fig. 2e and S8) [55], indicated the incorporation Au and Ag+, resulted in a tighter binding of Au, thereby reducing the electron density of Au and improving stability [56]. The binding energy at 368.3 eV and 374.3 eV were derived from Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 (Fig. S9) [57], and the peak of the Ag 3d5/2 spectrum was separated into Ag(I) at 368.3 eV and Ag(0) at 369.6 eV. Similarly, the Ag 3d3/2 spectrum was assigned to Ag(I) at 374.3 eV and Ag(0) at 375.6 eV. The above XPS curve fitting results indicated a portion of the Ag+ was reduced to Ag0 upon the introduction of Ag+ into NL-AuNCs. In conclusion, surficial Ag+ was reduced by Au0 and deposited on the surface of AuNCs, filling surface defects, increasing the number of luminescent AuNCs, and thereby enhancing their fluorescence intensity and stability [41].

To further evaluate the practicality and viability of NL-AuNCs utilizing as a fluorescent sensor for Ag+ detection, various real water samples, including tap water, Songhua River water, and Aershan water were selected for practical analysis. The aim was to determine whether other substances commonly found in natural ecosystems would interfere with the detection process to a certain extent. To accomplish this goal, Ag+ was introduced to diverse authentic water samples and diluted to two distinct concentration levels: 0.35 μM and 1.00 μM. Subsequently, the established standard method was implemented for comprehensive research and analysis. As shown in Table 1 , the detected concentration results of Ag+ in real water samples were consistent with the values obtained from the preparation of standard solutions. The recovery rate was attained ranged from 94.8 % to 98.8 %, with an RSD not exceeding 4.8 %. To validate the reliability of the constructed method, the actual samples with added Ag+ were tested by ICP-OES. The results demonstrated that the spiked concentrations detected by ICP-OES were close to the calibration samples, indicating the exceptional accuracy of the constructed method (Fig. S10). Therefore, the efficient, environmentally friendly, rapid, and simple NL-AuNCs fluorescence sensor, based on the one-pot preparation proposed in this work, held great promise for analyzing and detecting Ag+ in natural ecological water samples.

In summary, a smartphone-based NL-AuNCs fluorescent sensor has been devised, exhibiting remarkable advantages of rapidness, visualization, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity for the detection of Ag+ in environmental water samples. The addition of Ag+ effectively intensifies the fluorescence of NL-AuNCs via the AIE mechanism, the phenomenon substantiated by TEM images, fluorescence lifetime measurements. Additionally, both fluorescence spectra analysis and visual analysis by a smartphone platform exhibited superior potential for the detection of Ag+ with an ultralow limit of 1.3 nM, offering outstanding advantages of precision, which was expected to apply in in-situ sensing and monitoring in the environment. This will pave the way for monitoring the Ag+ in the future, enabling broader and more impactful use in various fields. And this work provided a feasible design for a promising portable sensing strategy.
